title: Differential Effects of Early Hippocampal Pathology on Episodic and Semantic Memory
research_question: Does early bilateral hippocampal pathology impair memory for personally experienced episodes and acquired semantic knowledge to the same degree?
method: A neuropsychological case series combined structural magnetic-resonance characterization with assessments of everyday episodic memory, language, literacy, and factual knowledge.
population_or_data: Three people whose brain injuries occurred at birth, by age four, or by age nine and whose imaging showed bilateral hippocampal pathology.
core_finding: All three cases had profound difficulty retaining everyday episodes while still acquiring speech, literacy, and factual knowledge in the low-average to average range, supporting a partial dissociation between episodic and semantic learning after early injury.
architecture_implications: Represent event-specific records and generalized semantic claims in distinct stores with separate update and retrieval paths.
Keep semantic abstractions linked to the episodes that support them; fluent factual access is not evidence that a particular episode can be recollected.
limitations: This is a three-case developmental lesion study. Early plasticity, heterogeneous injury histories, and tissue outside the hippocampus limit localization and generalization to typical adult memory.

search_queries: "Differential Effects of Early Hippocampal Pathology on Episodic and Semantic Memory" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Differential effects of early hippocampal pathology"

title: Preserved Learning and Retention of Pattern-Analyzing Skill in Amnesia: Dissociation of Knowing How and Knowing That
research_question: Can people with amnesia acquire and retain a rule-based perceptual skill despite impaired declarative memory?
method: Amnesic patients and matched controls repeatedly practiced mirror reading, and retention of the acquired skill was assessed over a period extending to three months.
population_or_data: Amnesic patients and matched control participants completing a mirror-reading skill task.
core_finding: Amnesic participants improved at mirror reading at a rate comparable to controls and retained the skill for at least three months, despite their declarative-memory impairment.
architecture_implications: Store learned procedures or policies separately from declarative event and fact records so that one system can improve without implying intact access in another.
Do not infer an explicit rationale or remembered learning episode solely from repeated successful behavior.
limitations: The clinical sample and one perceptual skill constrain generalization across procedural memory. Lesion etiologies and the abstract-level report do not establish one precise neural implementation.

search_queries: "Preserved learning and retention of pattern-analyzing skill in amnesia" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Preserved learning and retention of pattern-analyzing skill in amnesia"

title: A Neostriatal Habit Learning System in Humans
research_question: Do gradual habit learning and declarative memory depend on separable human memory systems?
method: Amnesic patients, nondemented patients with Parkinson's disease, and comparison participants performed a probabilistic classification task and were tested for declarative memory of the training episode.
population_or_data: Clinical groups with amnesia or Parkinson's disease plus comparison participants learning probabilistic cue-outcome associations.
core_finding: Amnesic participants learned the probabilistic classification while remembering little of training, whereas participants with Parkinson's disease remembered training but learned the classification poorly, yielding a double dissociation.
architecture_implications: Maintain a gradual habit or policy learner alongside an explicit episode-and-fact store, and expose which subsystem changed a recommendation.
Behavioral regularities accumulated over many interactions should not silently overwrite an explicit owner statement.
limitations: The inference relies on clinical-group dissociations and one probabilistic task; pathology, treatment, and broader group differences make the neural assignment less specific than a controlled intervention.

search_queries: "A neostriatal habit learning system in humans" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "A neostriatal habit learning system in humans"

title: The Capacity of Visual Working Memory for Features and Conjunctions
research_question: Is visual working-memory capacity limited by the number of individual features or by the number of integrated objects?
method: Visual change-detection experiments varied the number of colors, orientations, and multi-feature conjunctions participants had to retain across a brief delay.
population_or_data: Human participants remembering briefly presented arrays of simple visual objects.
core_finding: Performance was consistent with retaining roughly four integrated objects: adding features to each retained object was less costly than increasing the number of objects.
architecture_implications: Organize the active workspace around coherent event or entity bundles rather than treating every field or token as an independent slot.
Make workspace capacity, eviction, and overflow visible, while treating the reported object limit as a design analogy rather than a literal software constant.
limitations: Brief visual arrays are far removed from language-rich personal memory, and later work has refined capacity estimates and the role of attention. The study does not justify a universal four-item limit.

search_queries: "The capacity of visual working memory for features and conjunctions" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "The capacity of visual working memory for features and conjunctions"

title: The Episodic Buffer: A New Component of Working Memory?
research_question: What additional component is needed for working memory to bind information across specialized subsystems and long-term memory?
method: A theoretical revision of the multicomponent working-memory model synthesized behavioral, neuropsychological, developmental, and neuroimaging phenomena that the earlier three-component account did not readily explain.
population_or_data: Previously reported evidence from healthy adults, developmental studies, neuropsychological cases, and neuroimaging research.
core_finding: The paper proposes a limited-capacity episodic buffer that temporarily binds multimodal information and long-term knowledge into a unified representation available to executive control.
architecture_implications: Provide an ephemeral binding workspace that can join the current utterance, retrieved episodes, semantic claims, and goals without collapsing their source identities.
Keep durable storage separate from this buffer so that transient attention does not become an unreviewed long-term memory update.
limitations: This is a conceptual synthesis rather than a single direct test, and assumptions about conscious access and component boundaries remain model commitments rather than settled mechanisms.

search_queries: "The episodic buffer: a new component of working memory?" DOI PubMed
working memory episodic buffer primary paper Baddeley 2000

title: Normal Aging and Prospective Memory
venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
research_question: Is remembering to perform a future action especially impaired in older adults, and how does it relate to retrospective memory?
method: Two experiments embedded an intended action behind a target event, compared younger and older adults, measured three retrospective-memory tasks, and manipulated external aids and target familiarity.
population_or_data: Younger and older adults completing prospective and retrospective laboratory memory tasks.
core_finding: Older adults showed deficits on retrospective measures but not on the prospective task; prospective performance also improved with external aids and unfamiliar target events and did not reliably covary with retrospective performance.
architecture_implications: Represent intentions as a distinct memory type with explicit trigger conditions, completion state, and optional external reminders.
Do not estimate prospective reliability from ordinary recall alone; test whether the cue is detectable in the environment where the intention must fire.
limitations: The no-age-deficit result is tied to two laboratory tasks with salient event cues and should not be generalized to all real-world intentions, especially time-based or self-initiated ones.

search_queries: "Normal aging and prospective memory" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Normal aging and prospective memory"

title: Multiple Processes in Prospective Memory Retrieval: Factors Determining Monitoring Versus Spontaneous Retrieval
research_question: Can prospective intentions be retrieved both through resource-demanding monitoring and through spontaneous cue-triggered retrieval?
method: Five experiments measured costs to an ongoing task while participants held prospective intentions; the final experiment directly tested retrieval in conditions designed to reveal spontaneous remembering.
population_or_data: Human participants performing ongoing tasks with embedded event-based prospective-memory targets.
core_finding: Prospective remembering did not require one universal mechanism: good performance could arise through spontaneous cue-triggered retrieval, while task demands and individual differences changed reliance on active monitoring.
architecture_implications: Support both event-driven intention triggers and an explicit monitoring mode, recording which route caused an intention to surface.
Expose monitoring cost and permit an external-reminder fallback when trigger distinctiveness or available attention is low.
limitations: The evidence comes from short, controlled tasks with artificial targets. Ongoing-task cost is an indirect monitoring measure, and spontaneous retrieval in daily life may depend on richer contexts.

search_queries: "Multiple processes in prospective memory retrieval" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Multiple processes in prospective memory retrieval"

title: Aging and Source Monitoring
research_question: Does aging produce a general source-memory deficit, or does the deficit depend on how similar the candidate sources are?
method: One experiment compared younger and older adults on discriminations between external and self-generated memories, between two internally generated sources, and between two external sources.
population_or_data: Younger and older adults completing verbal source-monitoring tasks.
core_finding: Older adults were less accurate when distinguishing two sources from the same broad class, but not when distinguishing internally generated from externally presented memories, arguing against a uniform source deficit.
architecture_implications: Store source identity and source class with every memory, and estimate uncertainty from confusability among plausible same-class sources.
A source-memory warning should identify the ambiguous candidates rather than applying a blanket reliability penalty to a person or age group.
limitations: A single verbal experiment cannot establish a universal age effect, and its internal-versus-external source classes are much simpler than forwarded, quoted, inferred, and directly observed digital evidence.

search_queries: "Aging and source monitoring" 1989 DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Aging and source monitoring"

title: Reconsolidation of Episodic Memories: A Subtle Reminder Triggers Integration of New Information
research_question: Does reactivating a consolidated episodic memory make it susceptible to delayed incorporation of later information?
method: Across three multi-day experiments, undergraduates learned one object list, received a contextual reminder or no reminder before a second list, and later recalled either the first or second list; an immediate-test experiment assessed the time course.
population_or_data: Ninety-six University of Arizona undergraduates across three experiments using two lists of twenty unrelated objects.
core_finding: A subtle reminder before the second list caused delayed intrusions from the second list into recall of the first; the effect was absent on an immediate test and did not reverse when the second list was tested, consistent with time-dependent updating of the reactivated memory.
architecture_implications: Treat retrieval-driven updating as a versioned transaction: preserve the pre-update record, the reactivation cue, the new evidence, and the resulting derived version.
Run source-confusion and contradiction checks before consolidating new material into an existing episode; reactivation is an update opportunity, not a truth guarantee.
limitations: The experiments used artificial object lists and undergraduates. Reminder, experimenter, and room context differed between conditions, and a reconsolidation account is not the only possible explanation for delayed source intrusions.

search_queries: "Reconsolidation of episodic memories: A subtle reminder triggers integration of new information"
site:pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov PMC1838545 reconsolidation episodic memories

title: Odor Cues During Slow-Wave Sleep Prompt Declarative Memory Consolidation
research_question: Can a cue associated with learning selectively reactivate and strengthen declarative memory during slow-wave sleep?
method: An odor present during learning was re-presented during slow-wave sleep, rapid-eye-movement sleep, or wakefulness, with declarative and procedural retention measured and hippocampal response assessed by functional imaging.
population_or_data: Healthy adult participants completing hippocampus-dependent declarative and hippocampus-independent procedural memory tasks across sleep and wake conditions.
core_finding: Re-presenting the learning-associated odor during slow-wave sleep improved declarative retention and engaged the hippocampus; the same cue did not improve procedural memory and was ineffective during rapid-eye-movement sleep, wakefulness, or without prior odor-learning association.
architecture_implications: Make offline consolidation selective and cue-linked rather than an indiscriminate rewrite of all recent records.
Log which memories were replayed, the cue or policy that selected them, their memory class, and any post-consolidation change.
limitations: The laboratory odor and sleep-stage manipulation does not establish how an artificial system should schedule offline replay. The tasks and sample are narrow, and the abstract-level inspection does not support a universal sleep mechanism.

search_queries: "Odor cues during slow-wave sleep prompt declarative memory consolidation" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Odor cues during slow-wave sleep prompt declarative memory consolidation"

title: Remembering Can Cause Forgetting: Retrieval Dynamics in Long-Term Memory
venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
research_question: Does selectively retrieving some members of a category reduce later access to related but unpracticed memories?
method: Three studies had participants learn eight semantic categories, selectively retrieve half the members of half the categories, and later recall all items while controls addressed output interference.
population_or_data: Human experimental participants learning and retrieving categorized word lists.
core_finding: Selective retrieval improved practiced items but impaired later recall of related unpracticed high-frequency items; the impairment survived output-interference controls and remained after a delay of at least twenty minutes.
architecture_implications: Audit retrieval frequency and exposure because retrieval policy can alter which competing memories remain accessible.
Use retrieval diversity checks and competitor-aware evaluation so repeatedly surfaced claims do not crowd out plausible alternatives; mark low accessibility separately from falsity.
limitations: The effect was demonstrated with categorized words, was selective to stronger category members, and was measured over short delays. The proposed inhibitory mechanism has continuing boundary-condition debates.

search_queries: "Remembering can cause forgetting: Retrieval dynamics in long-term memory" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Remembering can cause forgetting: retrieval dynamics"

title: Replication and Analysis of Ebbinghaus' Forgetting Curve
research_question: Can Ebbinghaus' savings-based forgetting curve be closely replicated, and what functional shape best describes retention across minutes to a month?
method: A prerehearsed single participant spent about seventy hours learning and relearning Dutch-compatible nonsense-syllable lists after intervals from twenty minutes to thirty-one days; savings, serial position, and multiple curve families were analyzed.
population_or_data: One 22-year-old male participant, the second author, learning sixty-nine experimental lists over seventy-five days.
core_finding: The replication broadly reproduced rapid early loss followed by slower decline, while analyses suggested the curve was not perfectly smooth and might include an upward deviation near twenty-four hours.
architecture_implications: Learn retention and archival policies from item- and owner-specific evidence instead of imposing one universal linear or exponential decay rule.
Preserve access history and relearning savings as distinct signals; failure to freely retrieve does not prove that all residual information is gone.
limitations: This was a one-person self-experiment with artificial syllables, variable scheduling, and ten planned replications per interval, with nine at the 9-hour interval. The apparent twenty-four-hour increase does not by itself identify sleep as its cause.

search_queries: site:journals.plos.org "Replication and Analysis of Ebbinghaus' Forgetting Curve"
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Replication and Analysis of Ebbinghaus' Forgetting Curve"

title: The Critical Importance of Retrieval for Learning
research_question: After an item can first be recalled, do additional study and additional retrieval contribute equally to long-term learning?
method: University students learned foreign-language vocabulary under repeated study-test schedules that independently dropped successfully recalled items from further study, testing, both, or neither; delayed recall and performance predictions were compared.
population_or_data: University students learning foreign-language vocabulary pairs.
core_finding: Additional retrieval after initial success produced much better delayed recall, whereas additional study did not; students' predictions did not track their later performance.
architecture_implications: Use active owner retrieval or verification prompts, not passive re-exposure alone, when testing whether a personal fact remains usable.
Maintain calibrated memory confidence from observed retrieval outcomes separately from model fluency or the owner's immediate feeling of knowing.
limitations: The task involved vocabulary pairs in a controlled educational setting. It measured retention of practiced targets, not effects on related competitors or fidelity of autobiographical reconstruction.

search_queries: "The critical importance of retrieval for learning" DOI Science
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov 10.1126/science.1152408

title: Context-Dependent Memory in Two Natural Environments: On Land and Underwater
research_question: Does matching the physical environment at learning and recall improve free retrieval independently of disruption caused by changing environments?
method: Divers learned word lists either on land or underwater and recalled them in the same or alternate setting; a second experiment tested whether the act of changing settings explained the effect.
population_or_data: Divers learning and freely recalling word lists in natural land and underwater settings.
core_finding: Word lists were recalled better when the retrieval environment matched the learning environment, and a follow-up experiment made simple disruption from moving between settings an unlikely explanation.
architecture_implications: Retain encoding context as retrieval metadata and allow context-match features to influence ranking without making them mandatory gates.
When the current context differs sharply from the source context, surface lower retrieval confidence and broaden the search rather than declaring the memory absent.
limitations: The sample was specialized, the contexts were unusually distinct, and the task used short word lists. Ordinary conversational context effects may be smaller and less stable.

title: Illusions of Competence in Monitoring One's Knowledge During Study
venue: Journal of Experimental Psychology: Learning, Memory, and Cognition
research_question: Why can judgments of learning made during study overestimate what will later be retrievable from the cue alone?
method: Paired-associate experiments compared judgments made while cue and target were visible with later cue-only recall, manipulating properties of cue-target associations that differentially affect perceived and retrievable association strength.
population_or_data: Human participants studying and later recalling paired associates.
core_finding: Learners' confidence was influenced by associations apparent while both cue and target were present, even when those associations did not make the target retrievable from the cue at test.
architecture_implications: Calibrate memory confidence under the same information conditions in which retrieval will be required, including tests where the answer and supporting passage are hidden.
Treat fluency in the presence of evidence as distinct from unaided retrievability and from the truth of the underlying claim.
limitations: Paired-word judgments are a narrow form of metacognition, and the study does not show that all confidence reports are biased in the same direction or by the same cues.

search_queries: "Illusions of competence in monitoring one's knowledge during study" PubMed DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Illusions of competence in monitoring one's knowledge during study"

title: Evidence That Judgments of Learning Are Causally Related to Study Choice
research_question: Do judgments of learning directly determine what people choose to restudy, even when those judgments are experimentally dissociated from actual recall?
method: Three experiments manipulated repetition history and associative direction to separate judgments of learning from recall, then measured item-level restudy choices; one condition delayed the judgment.
population_or_data: Human participants learning paired associates and choosing items for further study.
core_finding: Restudy choices followed mistaken immediate judgments of learning rather than actual performance; when delayed judgments removed the bias, the corresponding study-choice bias also disappeared.
architecture_implications: Do not allocate consolidation or owner-interview effort from self-confidence alone; combine confidence with observed retrieval error, recency, stakes, and source quality.
Provide delayed or cue-reduced calibration checks before an active-learning scheduler decides that a memory no longer needs attention.
limitations: The choice environment and materials were constrained laboratory tasks. Delayed judgments reduced this bias in one paradigm but are not a complete metacognitive-control solution.

search_queries: "Evidence that judgments of learning are causally related to study choice"
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov "Evidence that judgments of learning are causally related to study choice"

title: Why There Are Complementary Learning Systems in the Hippocampus and Neocortex: Insights from the Successes and Failures of Connectionist Models of Learning and Memory
research_question: Why would a learning system need fast episodic storage and slow interleaved learning rather than one shared learning rate?
method: A theoretical and computational synthesis examined connectionist successes and catastrophic interference, relating model behavior to neuropsychological evidence about recent and remote memory.
population_or_data: Connectionist simulations and previously reported human and animal memory findings.
core_finding: Rapid storage protects new episodes from immediate interference, while gradual interleaved learning can extract shared structure without disrupting established representations; replay links the fast and slow systems over time.
architecture_implications: Use an append-oriented fast episodic journal plus a slower semantic consolidation process instead of rewriting a global owner model after every interaction.
Interleave candidate updates with representative older evidence and test for interference before promotion to durable semantic memory.
limitations: This is a foundational framework and review, not a single decisive experiment. Its biological mapping and account of remote memory are simplified, and later evidence has refined both systems and their interactions.

search_queries: "Why There Are Complementary Learning Systems in the Hippocampus and Neocortex" DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/7624455 "Why there are complementary learning systems"

title: Complementary Learning Systems Within the Hippocampus: A Neural Network Modelling Approach to Reconciling Episodic Memory with Statistical Learning
venue: Philosophical Transactions of the Royal Society B: Biological Sciences
research_question: Can one hippocampal model rapidly memorize individual episodes and extract temporal regularities without forcing both computations through the same representations?
method: A biologically motivated network modeled entorhinal cortex, dentate gyrus, CA3, and CA1 pathways; five hundred randomized networks learned paired and community-structured sequences, with pathway lesions and representational analyses testing function.
population_or_data: Computational simulations across five hundred random network initializations per analysis using synthetic temporal sequences.
core_finding: In the model, the monosynaptic entorhinal-CA1 pathway learned statistical regularities, while the sparse trisynaptic pathway retained separated episodes; recurrence supported higher-order relational behavior.
architecture_implications: Within the fast-memory tier, separate pattern-separated episode records from a regularity-learning path rather than forcing one representation to optimize both specificity and generalization.
Test architecture claims with ablations that remove replay, episode storage, or abstraction paths and report which behaviors fail.
limitations: The inputs and anatomy are simplified and conclusions depend on learning rules, rates, and lesion definitions chosen by the modelers. This is computational evidence, not direct demonstration of the proposed division in a digital or human system.

search_queries: "Complementary learning systems within the hippocampus" DOI PMC
site:pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov PMC5124075 complementary learning systems hippocampus

title: An Integrated Theory of the Mind
research_question: How can specialized perceptual, motor, goal, and declarative-memory modules be coordinated into coherent cognition within ACT-R?
method: The paper specifies a formal modular architecture with buffers, production-rule selection, and subsymbolic parameters, then illustrates module-level and integrated behavior against multiple empirical tasks.
population_or_data: ACT-R computational models and selected human behavioral and neuroimaging results used as demonstrations.
core_finding: ACT-R coordinates specialized modules by exposing their contents in limited buffers to a production system that selects one rule at a time, while subsymbolic processes guide selection and learning.
architecture_implications: Make episodic or declarative memory, procedures, goals, perception, and action explicit modules with typed interfaces rather than one opaque prompt state.
Record buffer contents, candidate actions, the selected production or policy, and subsymbolic scores so a decision can be reconstructed after the fact.
limitations: ACT-R is a broad theory illustrated by selected tasks, not evidence of cognitive equivalence or a validated person model. Its cortical mappings are coarse and many implementation choices remain task-specific.

search_queries: "An integrated theory of the mind" ACT-R DOI
site:pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/15482072 "An integrated theory of the mind"

title: A Large-Scale Model of the Functioning Brain
research_question: Can one large-scale neurally grounded model integrate perception, working memory, rule-like processing, and motor output across multiple tasks?
method: The authors built Spaun, a 2.5-million-neuron spiking model receiving visual image sequences and responding through a physical arm model, and demonstrated it on eight distinct tasks.
population_or_data: A computational neural model evaluated on eight perception, memory, reasoning, and action tasks.
core_finding: One integrated model used common neural mechanisms and interfaces to perform eight tasks spanning perception, sequence memory, simple reasoning, and action rather than presenting a separate model for each task.
architecture_implications: Evaluate an inspectable digital brain as an integrated system with shared typed representations and end-to-end task traces, not only as isolated memory benchmarks.
Treat successful task integration as engineering feasibility evidence while preserving modular state and ablation hooks for diagnosis.
limitations: Spaun is simplified, computationally expensive, and demonstrated on a fixed set of constrained tasks. It lacks autobiographical, social, prospective, and owner-correction demands and does not establish human cognitive equivalence.

search_queries: "A large-scale model of the functioning brain" DOI Science
